tweets.text:
Physical AI is one of the defining investment themes of the next decade

An hour ago, Volkswagen announced it was cutting 50k jobs... that's 8% of its workforce!

Global manufacturers are under pressure to rebuild production around automation, lower costs and AI-driven efficiency

That doesn't mean humanoids suddenly replace every worker, or at least not overnight

Industrial robots, autonomous mobile robots & humanoids solve different problems and all three markets can compound together

The difference today is that humanoids are finally becoming commercially viable:

- Mercedes has invested in Apptronik and is trialling Apollo (its flagship humanoid) on production lines
- BMW is deploying humanoids after successful factory pilots in Europe

I've been spending more time researching robotics because I think we're watching the emergence of a completely dominant tech stack

... and if autonomous machines become economic participants, crypto starts looking less like a payments network for humans and more like infrastructure for machines with identity, ownership, coordination and value transfer

Expect the robotics insight cadence to ramp up over the coming months, particularly as we examine each area of the robotics stack for opportunities!
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
One of the strongest early signals for any investment thesis is when the private portfolio begins repricing before the wider market has fully recognised the opportunity

XMAQUINA has built verified positions across some of the most important private humanoid robotics companies, https://t.co/Pt2s9tEUM7

tweets.text:
It's been two months since @maraoz made this DeFi doom call. Since then, GLM 5.2, Fable, and GPT 5.6 have all shipped and are all being used in the wild by attackers.

The data is in. It's time to call it: the DeFi "hackpocalypse" was a false alarm.

It's more than half-way through the year and annualized $ hacked in DeFi in 2026 is lower than 2025 year, and well within historical range.

The deeper story is that while the NUMBER of hacks has spiked, the SIZE of hacks fell even more. This means attackers are picking off small protocols and abandonware, the ones that can't afford to AI-harden their code. But large protocols have done the AI-hardening, and they're actually pretty secure now.

Lesson: the average dollar in DeFi is as safe as it was a year ago. If you keep your money in large protocols that can afford to harden themselves, you'll likely be fine.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
PSA: I now consider *all* of DeFi unsafe.

Coding agents are superhuman at finding vulnerabilities, and smart contract security is too asymmetric: defenders need to fix every bug while attackers need just one exploit to steal funds.

thread_tweets.text:
In case you don't believe me:

1. Comparing 2025 & 2026 w/ outlier months removed, shows that even without the outlier months (Bybit hack vs Drift & KelpDAO), 2026 is STILL a lower rate of $ hacked per month
2. Median hack size by year is also declining, showing that we're moving more toward smaller hacks of smaller protocols
3. Some have claimed this is because hacks are moving into admin key hacks; admin key / multisig hacks are *already included* in this dataset. Those are going down in dollar terms as well.

tweets.full_text:
It's been two months since @maraoz made this DeFi doom call. Since then, GLM 5.2, Fable, and GPT 5.6 have all shipped and are all being used in the wild by attackers.

The data is in. It's time to call it: the DeFi "hackpocalypse" was a false alarm.

It's more than half-way through the year and annualized $ hacked in DeFi in 2026 is lower than 2025 year, and well within historical range.

The deeper story is that while the NUMBER of hacks has spiked, the SIZE of hacks fell even more. This means attackers are picking off small protocols and abandonware, the ones that can't afford to AI-harden their code. But large protocols have done the AI-hardening, and they're actually pretty secure now.

Lesson: the average dollar in DeFi is as safe as it was a year ago. If you keep your money in large protocols that can afford to harden themselves, you'll likely be fine.

In case you don't believe me:

1. Comparing 2025 & 2026 w/ outlier months removed, shows that even without the outlier months (Bybit hack vs Drift & KelpDAO), 2026 is STILL a lower rate of $ hacked per month
2. Median hack size by year is also declining, showing that we're moving more toward smaller hacks of smaller protocols
3. Some have claimed this is because hacks are moving into admin key hacks; admin key / multisig hacks are *already included* in this dataset. Those are going down in dollar terms as well.
